questions.question: Explain the difference between unary and binary operator overloading with examples.
keyPoints: Unary operators work on single operand (++, --, -, !)
Binary operators work on two operands (+, -, *, /)
Unary can be member function with no parameters
Binary as member takes one parameter, as friend takes two
Example: operator-() for negation, operator+(...) for addition

questions.question: Write the syntax and rules for overloading the subscript operator [].
keyPoints: ReturnType operator[](int index)
Should return reference for modification
Two versions needed: const and non-const
Used for array-like access
Example: int& operator[](int i) { return arr[i]; }

questions.question: Explain why the assignment operator should return by reference.
keyPoints: Returns *this for chain assignment
a = b = c works because b=c returns reference to b
Returning by value breaks chaining
Also enables usage in expressions like if((a=b)>0)
C++ standard requires this behavior for consistency

questions.question: Discuss the overloaded && and || operators and their limitations in C++.
keyPoints: && and || can be overloaded
Lose short-circuit evaluation property
Become regular function calls with different semantics
Both operands are always evaluated
Not recommended to overload these operators

questions.question: What is a conversion operator? How is it defined in C++? Give an example.
keyPoints: Converts class object to another type
Syntax: operator TypeName() const { return value; }
No return type in definition
Must be member function
Example: operator int() const { return real; }

questions.question: Explain the difference between prefix and postfix ++ operators.
keyPoints: Prefix: operator++() - increments then returns reference to this
Postfix: operator++(int) - takes dummy int parameter
Postfix returns copy BEFORE increment (by value)
Prefix returns reference to modified object
Prefix is more efficient (no copy needed)

questions.question: Write a C++ class with necessary operator overloading to add two complex numbers.
keyPoints: Class Complex with real, imag members
Constructor (default and parameterized)
operator+ as member or friend function
operator<< for output using friend
Returns new Complex with added values

questions.question: Design a complete C++ class for a 2D Point with necessary operator overloading: assignment, addition, subtraction, comparison, and stream insertion.
keyPoints: Private members: x, y coordinates
Constructors (default, parameterized, copy)
operator= returning *this by reference
operator+ and operator- for point operations
operator== and operator!= for comparison
operator<< as friend for stream output
Handle self-assignment in operator=
Proper const correctness throughout

questions.question: Explain all the rules and restrictions that must be followed when overloading operators in C++.
keyPoints: Cannot overload built-in operators only user-defined
At least one operand must be user-defined type
Cannot change arity (unary remains unary)
Cannot change precedence and associativity
Cannot create new operators
Some operators cannot be overloaded (::, ., .*, ?:)
Most can be overloaded as member or friend
Cannot overload for primitive types directly
Operators maintain original semantics

questions.question: Write a C++ program to implement a String class with operator overloading for concatenation (+), comparison (==, !=, <), and assignment. Also explain the memory management considerations.
keyPoints: String class with char* or string data member
Constructor, destructor, copy constructor
operator+ for concatenation returning new String
operator== and operator!= for equality
operator< for lexicographic comparison
operator= handling self-assignment and deep copy
operator[] for character access
Consider Rule of Three/Five for proper memory management

questions.question: Explain the concept of friend functions for operator overloading. When and why are friend functions preferred over member functions? Give suitable examples.
keyPoints: Friend functions have access to private members
Not bound to 'this' pointer
Preferred when left operand is built-in type
Required for << and >> operators
Example: cout << obj needs friend
Can make both operands symmetric
Syntax: friend ReturnType operator+(const Type&, const Type&)
